summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PARENTING – Interim hearing with respect to pressing parenting issues pending further interim hearing – Where the impending holiday period created some urgency – Where the matter has been set down for a further interim hearing – Orders made for the children to spend time with the mother in circumstances of agreement between the parents – Orders made commencing a process of therapeutic intervention between the mother and one of the children.

subseq.en: Standard tier (auto-summary). May be upgraded to a full deep-dive later.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

SIMPSON J:

This is an interim application concerning the parties’ two children, X and Y. The children live with the father.

BACKGROUND

Because of events that occurred in late 2025 involving X, the mother has been charged with certain criminal offences, the hearing of which is next listed before the Local Court of New South Wales in mid-2026. The mother has not spent time with X since 6 December 2025. The mother’s time with Y also changed after the December events but, more recently, time has resumed, though on terms different to those which had been in place during the latter part of 2025.
Each of the children have a congenital medical condition which requires daily medication for management. The parents identify that X has behavioural difficulties and he has been diagnosed with ADHD (Combined) but, at least at the time when the report interviews occurred, had not commenced a trial of medication (Exhibit 4 at [18]).
This matter was listed for final hearing on 28 April – 1 May, 8 and 28 May 2026. At a compliance hearing on 16 January 2026, I raised my concerns about the matter proceeding on those dates in circumstances where the mother was facing serious criminal charges (which are the second set of charges against the mother relating to X), the hearing of which would not have concluded prior to the final hearing in this Court. At that time, the mother was not legally represented, and the matter was adjourned to enable the mother to secure legal advice and to give consideration to the making of an application to vacate the final hearing dates.
On 6 March 2026, the mother filed an Application in a Proceeding in which she sought a range of orders with respect to her care of the children.
The matter was listed on 13 March 2026 for case management of the mother’s application for spend time arrangements with the children. Directions were made that day for the father to file a Response to an Application in a Proceeding and evidence by a particular date, and other directions with respect to preparation for an interim hearing.
The matter was adjourned that day to 23 March 2026 for interim hearing, with one hour being allocated for that hearing in circumstances where some urgency related to the application, relating to spend time arrangements for the children during the imminent first term school holidays.
The mother’s evidence made clear that she had family members visiting this country from Country C and that she identified benefits to both children in spending time with those family members, and she sought orders about that. Given the specific dates of the visit, some urgency arose relating to the hearing of the mother’s application for time between the children and the maternal family.
Following the directions hearing, on 13 March 2026, the mother filed a further Application in a Proceeding (sealed on 16 March 2026) in which she sought to vacate the final hearing dates and for new dates to be allocated on a date after mid-2026, when the criminal proceedings are, she anticipates, likely to be determined.
On 17 March 2026, I had my Associate write to the parties to confirm that the interim hearing (listed on 23 March 2026) would regard both Applications in a Proceeding filed by the mother and for that purpose the father could file a Response to Application in a Proceeding and affidavit addressing both applications.

The hearing on 23 March 2026

On 20 March 2026, the father filed an affidavit and two days later, a case outline document.
The minute of orders sought by the father (in his case outline document) made clear that for different reasons, he also sought that the final hearing dates be vacated. The father submitted in his case outline document that the adjournment of the final hearing would permit reconsideration of the time arrangements for the children with the mother, pending the final hearing, and allow time for additional therapeutic intervention, beneficial to X, to occur in that adjourned period.
At the hearing on 23 March 2026, including with the support of the Independent Children’s Lawyer (“the ICL”), it was resolved that the final hearing dates would be vacated and that the matter would again be listed for interim hearing on 27 March 2026, for one hour, to allow the parties to see what, if any, matters could be resolved by agreement in the interim period. Given that it was anticipated that additional time for the interim hearing might be required to address the less time-sensitive issues, the matter was also allocated time for hearing on 28 April 2026 (using one of the days previously allocated for the final hearing). New final hearing dates were provided to the parties, commencing in late September 2026.
It was expected that the parties might succeed in narrowing the issues and, or, identifying those matters which required the court’s urgent attention on the next occasion.

The hearing on 27 March 2026

The mother’s case outline document filed on 20 March 2026 identified the documents relied upon by the mother for the hearing. I granted the mother leave to rely upon the three brief affidavits she had prepared in circumstances where I was advised that each contained evidence relevant to the matters to be determined (including the affidavit filed on 20 March 2026, which included the mother’s response to matters submitted in the case outline document filed on behalf of the ICL earlier that day, in addition to affidavits filed on 6 March 2026 and 13 March 2026) and where no objection was taken to that approach.
With the issue of the adjournment of the final hearing date having been addressed on the previous occasion, the remaining issues identified by the mother as requiring determination were:
(a) Whether professionally supervised time between X and the mother should recommence in accordance with existing interim orders;

(b) Whether the interim care arrangements for Y should revert to arrangements that were agreed between the parties prior to 11 January 2026 (which the mother asserted provided for the child to spend seven nights a fortnight with each parent);

(c) Whether the mother should be permitted to attend school and sporting events involving the children on an interim basis; and

(d) Whether the children should spend time with the mother during the Term 1 school holidays to enable them to spend time with extended maternal family.

The father had failed to file a Response to an Application in a Proceeding, contrary to the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth) (“the Rules”) and despite the specific direction made on 13 March 2026 about this. Instead, the father had prepared a minute of orders sought which was included in his case outline document and which had been provided to the mother and the ICL on the morning of 23 March 2026. While the father’s solicitor cited “time pressures” to explain why a Response to an Application in a Proceeding had not been prepared, that explanation was difficult to reconcile with the fact that the time available had been sufficient to permit other documents to be prepared and filed, including an affidavit of the father and a minute of orders sought.
In any event, I granted leave to the father to rely upon the minute of orders sought as contained in his case outline document, notwithstanding his failure to comply with the Rules and the directions made, in circumstances where I was satisfied that the mother and the ICL had received the father’s case outline document.
The father contended, in effect, that the issues in dispute were:
(a) Whether the mother caring for X created an unacceptable risk of harm for X and what arrangements, if any, might be put in place to mitigate that risk;

(b) What arrangements should be put in place with respect to the mother caring for Y; and

(c) Whether the final hearing dates should be vacated (which became an agreed position at the commencement of the interim hearing on 23 March 2026).

Ms Kelly (“the Single Expert”), psychologist, was appointed as the Single Expert in this matter. A Short Form Report of the Single Expert dated 23 January 2025 became Exhibit 3 and her Single Expert Report dated 23 June 2025 became Exhibit 4.
I have had regard to both of those documents, in addition to a range of other documents which were tendered and made exhibits at the interim hearing.
In circumstances where limited time was available to hear the matter on 27 March 2026, I sought to obtain an understanding from the parties of those issues which remained in dispute and about which an urgent determination was required.
As a result of the submissions which occurred that morning, I understood that a range of matters had been resolved:
(a) By agreement between the parties, Y was again spending unsupervised, block time in her mother’s care. It was not clear to me whether that time had progressed to being in seven-day blocks as the mother sought or some other configuration, as the father sought. It was clear however, that the parents had managed to progress those matters between them and no submissions were made in support of particular orders about those time arrangements.

(b) It was agreed that the children would spend time with the mother’s extended family during their visit from Country C which would, as I understood the evidence, include time during the school holidays.

(c) Specific arrangements had been made for Y to spend a block of time with her mother during the school holidays (certain dates were submitted as agreed).

(d) At the time of the interim hearing, Y was in her mother’s care, in Victoria, having attended a family wedding.

(e) While the mother also wanted to spend time with X, in the context of the time he was spending with the extended family, she identified the priority of X having that time with his family and did not wish to jeopardise that occurring. It was submitted on behalf of the mother that, if necessary, she would remain absent from those arrangements to ensure the father was prepared to support the child spending time with the visiting family members.

CONSIDERATION

Professionally supervised time

The parties had previously agreed and included a number of the mother’s extended family members as being suitable to provide supervision of her time with X, as set out in orders made on 29 January 2025. I understand from the submissions made and the written evidence, that while the orders of 29 January 2025 limited X’s time with his mother, the parties had subsequently agreed different arrangements, in response to X’s desire to spend time with his mother. Those informal but consensual arrangements (which included, on the father’s evidence, unsupervised time and supervised time, including professionally supervised time) ceased after the events of December 2025 when X alleged that he had been physically assaulted by his mother and exposed to ridicule by her. The father asserts in his affidavit that X reported his experiences to people at his school and the mother was arrested consequent to those reports.
Those matters are presently not addressed by the mother in her evidence in circumstances where she wishes to protect her right to silence pending the hearing of those criminal charges.
The mother sought to progress the resumption of time with X and sought the restoration of professionally supervised time, as had been in place previously. That was opposed by the father and the ICL. In circumstances where the father and the ICL identified opposition by X to the reinstatement of professional supervision and where X’s vulnerability required the protective attention of both parents, I indicated that I would not be prepared to entertain that application (about professionally supervised time) at the time of the interim hearing, which was abridged in nature. I had regard to the feedback provided to the parents by X’s treating psychologist, Ms B (including in Exhibit 6), and her concerns about X’s vulnerabilities expressed in that document.
Exhibits 6 and 7 were notes of X’s counsellor and feedback which had been provided to both parents following sessions held with the child. Ms B records that X was opposed to immediately resuming time with his mother. She further noted that while resuming time between X and his mother was identified as beneficial to the child, the process of doing so required some caution.
Ms B had expressed to the parents her recommendations as to how the process might occur, including establishing a means by which a facilitated discussion between X and his mother might occur, as the first step in considering the resumption of time. The mother focussed upon Ms B’s suggestion (in her written feedback) that a single session might be sufficient to provide a foundation for the resumption of her time thereafter, with X. The father’s proposed orders contemplated that the process to be undertaken might involve a longer engagement, before time might resume.
In addition, the ICL referred to reports of the last professional supervisors assisting the family (Exhibit 10), reports which cover supervised contact sessions on 8 December 2024, 26 January 2025 and 2 February 2025. The report of the events on 2 February 2025 makes clear that X continued to exhibit obstructive and challenging behaviours towards his mother, at times his sister, and also to the supervisor attending that day. It was observed that shortly after the session commenced at the mother’s home, X locked the mother and the supervisors out of the home, and it was some time before he opened the door (and after a threat was made to call the police). Once inside, X made provocative comments to the mother, declaring that he did not wish to see her the following Tuesday and declaring “You still need a supervisor because you got arrested and you are a child abuser”. There were other instances of provocative and disruptive behaviour observed of X, noted in that report, along with observations of happy, loving and affectionate engagement between X and the mother. The father was telephoned during the visit, and he attended to speak with X and to assist the child in better regulating his behaviour.
The events which precipitated the suspension of the mother’s time with X (and further criminal charges) occurred in December 2025. I infer that X’s engagement with the mother (and with others) had continued to be challenging in the lead up to the December events.
While the ICL generally expressed support that X’s engagement with his mother (and his care more generally) would be assisted by continuing therapeutic engagement, the ICL submitted that responsibility lay with the parents to address their own circumstances as a precursor to further engagement for and with X. The ICL identified the continuing vulnerabilities of X and called upon the parents to do the preliminary self-work to better improve their abilities to support X and to embrace the recommendations of X’s psychologist, Ms B, as expressed in her feedback letter of 25 February 2026 (Exhibit 12).
In that letter, Ms B shares her opinion that X’s mental health vulnerabilities were such that he would best respond to spending time with his mother supervised “by a safe and familiar adult”. Ms B identified support for this approach:
(a) In the reports of the Department of Communities and Justice (“the DCJ”);

(b) X’s own preferences (that he would not again engage with professional supervisors given his perceptions of his experiences with the last service and his expectation that he may feel more vulnerable to emotional dysregulation if supervised by a stranger when he is feeling uncomfortable);

(c) As it is trauma informed (with X’s trauma responses reported by his paediatrician) and X’s sense of comfort and autonomy might be better supported in this way, allowing him to feel empowered and emotionally safe;

(d) It may better foster opportunities for reconnection if X felt more comfortable with people known to him acting as supervisors; and

(e) It reflected X’s preferences, as he reported to her, about spending time with his mother at her home, such that he might open gifts there and with the support of a nominated third party.

I also have regard to the father’s evidence that X has expressed suicidal ideation at school, as recently as 17 March 2026, following a session with Ms B when, the father contends, discussion occurred about the possible resumption of supervised time.
At this very abridged interim hearing, I place some weight upon the observations of Ms B, who has been entrusted by the parties to provide ongoing therapeutic care to X. I note the cautions she recommends about how to approach the practical aspects of the resumption of X’s time with his mother.
I indicated to the parties that the mother’s application relating to resuming professionally supervised time with X would not be further considered that day, given the time limitations and the more complex considerations that such an application warranted.

X’s time with the maternal family

On behalf of the father, a series of text messages between the parents and between the father and members of the mother’s family were exhibited (Exhibit 9). Those messages reveal a co‑operative engagement by the father and members of the mother’s family to create opportunities for X to spend time with the extended maternal family in the circumstances of their planned visit to this country. The father also indicates flexibility and willingness to make X available in particular locations, other than Sydney, to try to accommodate this occurring.
In the course of the interim hearing, it became clear that the parties had in fact agreed to time occurring between both children and the extended maternal family. To the extent that the mother also sought to be able to participate in those occasions when X was also present, the mother selflessly indicated that she would not insist on such occurring if it would jeopardise X’s opportunity to spend time with the wider family.
On review of the text messages exchanged between the parties and which form part of Exhibit 9, I identify a text exchange between the parents on 23 March 2026 (a mere four days prior to the interim hearing) in which the father records the mother as having told X that she was willing to spend time with him in the company of two (named) members of the mother’s family (and otherwise without professional supervision). The father reports that X indicated that he was agreeable to that approach and the father invited the mother to “confirm whether you agree, and we can explore options”.
In a return message sent at 7.10 pm that evening, the mother confirmed her support for that plan and indicated “subject to their availability, I am open to short, supervised time with [X] in their presence”.
In those circumstances, it appears that the parties have already directly agreed that the mother may spend some limited time (the mother described this as “short time”) with X in the presence of (named) family members. The fact of that agreement being reached between the parties was not the subject of direct submission by the representatives of the father or the mother at the interim hearing.
Because of the above, I am satisfied that the parties have, in a sensible and child focussed way, been able to agree arrangements for the children to spend time with the mother and, or, her extended family. I have regard to the Safety Plan agreed between the parties and the DCJ dated 19 December 2025, in which it was agreed by the parents that X would “have a break” from spending time with his mother at her home. It appears, based on the father’s texts to the mother, that X is now comfortable about some time with his mother with particular family members present.

DETERMINATION

Where I do not have the particulars of what has been agreed between the parties and where a minute reflecting the agreed circumstances between the parties has not been provided to the Court, I propose to make orders:
(a) Providing for X and Y to spend time with the mother’s extended family as agreed between the parties in writing;

(b) Permitting the mother to spend such time with X as is agreed between the parties in writing and subject to the supervision of an agreed person or persons; and

(c) Noting the agreement between the parties as to the resumption and continuation of the mother’s care of Y, that Y continue to live with the father and spend time with the mother as agreed between the parties. I note that I invited the parties to provide a minute of agreed orders to chambers if they sought to formalise the newly agreed arrangements for Y’s care, in particular. No such document has been provided at this time, but I will make an order granting liberty to the parties to approach Chambers should such be contemplated or should any issue or otherwise arise with respect to the further interim hearing.

In circumstances where the care arrangements for the children have been varied by agreement without further order, I was encouraged to discharge orders previously made which provide for the care arrangements for both X and Y (dated 29 January 2025 and 3 February 2025, respectively). On review of those orders, it is clear that there exists a number of further orders beyond those which provide for the time which the children are to spend with the mother. Therefore, at this stage, I do not propose to discharge those orders other than to make an order which discharges all previous orders which provide for the mother to spend time with the children. As per the Orders of 29 January 2025 and 3 February 2025, the children are to remain living with the father and the orders to that effect will remain.
The father contends in his affidavit that the mother continues to be subject to an Apprehended Domestic Violence Order (“ADVO”). While the ADVO was not tendered, I have had regard to that document. I accept that the ADVO remains current, and as set out in the ICL’s case outline document, remains in force for two years (to early 2027). The terms of the orders I propose to make (as general as they are) are not inconsistent with the terms of an ADVO in place for the protection of the children and the father, given the document permits arrangements to be agreed between the parents about the mother’s time and communication with the children.

Therapeutic intervention

The father also sought orders with respect to a mechanism for the parties to obtain from Ms B, her detailed recommendations as to the process by which X may be supported to re-engage with the mother. Ms B provided some feedback to the parties about this in her email to them of 27 February 2026 (part of Exhibit 6). The father has set out in his minute of orders sought, a detailed set of proposed orders to secure Ms B’s advice and then recommendations as to practitioners to assist the parties.
It would appear to be helpful and necessary for the parents to jointly seek from Ms B further information about the process she has identified as the “repair session” which she says ought to occur prior to X resuming in person time with his mother. I expect that the parties are capable of making those inquiries of Ms B as to who may be suitable to facilitate such a process, but in any event, I will make orders which make that process clear.
Upon receipt of further information from Ms B and after the parties select a suitable professional to support a safe and clinically guided re-establishment of X’s relationship with the mother as may be required and as per the orders I make, if the parties are unable to agree to “next steps”, those issues will be determined on the next occasion, at which time the court will have the benefit of further evidence about Ms B’s recommendations and submissions based on those recommendations.
The father’s solicitor advocate made submissions with respect to the risk to X of experiencing psychological harm in the event that time between X and his mother was to resume prior to the therapeutic “repair session” identified by Ms B. However, I am satisfied that the parents had reached agreement that X would spend time with the mother, for “a short time”, in the presence of agreed family members. That arrangement has the benefit of reflecting the kind of scaffolding which Ms B has identified is X’s preference with respect to how he might resume spending time with his mother.
Consequently, I do not accept that the exploration of these “next steps” with Ms B or another suitable professional should delay the mother and X resuming time together, in the company of other family, if such continues to be agreed between the parties as a preliminary means of facilitating X and the mother having some time together. The exploration and possible commencement of a therapeutic process to repair and aid resumption of the mother’s relationship with X may require some additional time and patience in order to be effective. The parents will have a better appreciation of those matters after the engagement with Ms B, as contemplated in these orders, is undertaken.
I also note the submissions of the ICL to the effect that the parties, as the adults responsible for the care of these children, might engage in self-reflection about the things which they each might do to improve X’s circumstances and experiences, rather than focus upon processes and change for X. Those submissions have additional weight in circumstances where, for example, the recent evidence of each of the parties does not reveal the steps either has taken in response to the recommendations expressed previously by the Single Expert or in response to orders previously made.
I will otherwise make directions for the filing of material prior to the upcoming interim hearing, such that the court has evidence with respect to the implementation of these orders and the relevant events which transpire, if any, over this adjourned period.
